narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48.2% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 32, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings both at 3, upper-middle family income, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average undergraduate GPA is 3.24 and LSAT is 37.27, with Year 3 deciles averaging 5.91, showing your metrics fall below those benchmarks. The three most influential factors in this decision were your undergraduate GPA, Year 3 decile, and LSAT score. Your undergraduate GPA had the strongest pull toward a passing prediction, as it suggested some foundational strength despite being lower than typical successful students. In contrast, your Year 3 decile ranking pushed notably toward a failure prediction, indicating your relative performance in later undergraduate years raised concerns compared to peers who passed. Your LSAT score also contributed toward failure, reinforcing worries about standardized reasoning skills essential for the bar. Your full-time enrollment plan aligns well with the 93% of passers who chose that path, and your upper-middle income is common among them at 45%, so those didn't sway the outcome much. Overall, these key academic indicators tipped the model toward rejection, even as your GPA offered some counterbalance. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and potential. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps—reapplying after targeted improvements could shift your predicted odds significantly. You're clearly driven, and with focused steps, a path to admission remains very possible.
